ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for iced lime milk; 'milk beverage' (FOODON_03301737) used as closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Milk-based drinks) used as closest parent; no specific code for iced lime milk exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226789007 (Milk drink) used as closest parent; no specific concept for iced lime milk.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard glass of iced lime milk, approximately 300 ml (300 g), as typically served in a Singapore café or Vietnamese-style restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Milk, whole, 3.25% milkfat, with added vitamin D (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FDC ID 1097512) combined with lime juice, raw (FDC ID 167747) in estimated proportions for iced lime milk
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Iced lime milk is not listed as a single entry in any primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for whole milk (FDC ID 1097512) and raw lime juice (FDC ID 167747), combined in approximate proportions of 75% milk and 25% lime juice by volume, with a small addition of sugar (~5 g per 100 g serving). This is a standard preparation estimate consistent with Vietnamese-style sữa đá chanh recipes. Values are per 100 g of the final beverage.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — milk (full-fat) GI reference; lime juice contribution negligible
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for iced lime milk. GI estimated at 32 based on full-fat milk GI of approximately 31–34 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2021). Lime juice has negligible glycemic contribution. Added sugar (~5 g per 100 g) slightly elevates GI above plain milk. GL per 300 g serving = (32 × 31.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 9.5 (rounded). Available carbohydrate per serving = 10.5 g/100 g × 300 g × 0.95 correction for fibre ≈ 31.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content; potassium and calcium present
hypertension.guidance: Iced lime milk contains approximately 38 mg sodium per 100 g, which is low, and provides potassium (145 mg) and calcium (88 mg) that may support blood pressure regulation; however, added sugar content should be monitored in hypertensive individuals managing metabolic risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar and total carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Iced lime milk has a low GI of approximately 32 due to the milk base, but the added sugar (~5 g per 100 g) and a glycemic load of approximately 9.5 per 300 g serving mean that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size and consider requesting reduced or no added sugar when ordering.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from whole milk; low sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Iced lime milk provides approximately 1.4 g saturated fat per 100 g from whole milk, which is moderate; individuals with cardiovascular disease may benefit from requesting a low-fat milk variant to reduce saturated fat intake while retaining the calcium and vitamin D benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Iced Lime Milk (Sữa Đá Chanh) is a refreshing cold beverage popular in Vietnamese cuisine and widely served in Singapore's Vietnamese-style restaurants and cafés. It is prepared by combining fresh whole milk with freshly squeezed lime juice, a small amount of sugar or sweetened condensed milk, and crushed or cubed ice. The result is a creamy, tangy, and lightly sweet drink with a frothy texture from the interaction of the acidic lime juice with the milk proteins. The beverage is typically served in a tall glass and consumed immediately. Nutritionally, iced lime milk provides moderate energy (approximately 72 kcal per 100 g), meaningful calcium (88 mg per 100 g), vitamin C from the lime juice (approximately 7.5 mg per 100 g), and vitamin B12 from the milk. Its glycemic index is low (approximately 32), making it a more moderate glycemic choice compared to sugar-sweetened soft drinks, though the added sugar content warrants attention for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The dish is vegetarian, halal-friendly, and gluten-free, but contains dairy and is not suitable for lactose-intolerant individuals. In Singapore, it is commonly found at Vietnamese restaurants such as Co Chung Restaurant and is priced at approximately SGD 10 per serving.
common_user_queries: How many calories in iced lime milk?
Is iced lime milk good for diabetics?
What is the GI of lime milk?
What is lime milk made of?

confidence_reason: Iced lime milk is not listed as a single entry in any primary regional approved source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for whole milk and raw lime juice in approximate proportions consistent with standard Vietnamese-style sữa đá chanh preparation. GI is estimated by analogy with full-fat milk from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (whole milk FDC ID 1097512 and lime juice FDC ID 167747) in approximate proportions of 75% milk, 25% lime juice, plus ~5 g added sugar per 100 g of final beverage. No single approved-source entry exists for this specific beverage. 2. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on full-fat milk GI (31–34) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0; trace amounts of naturally occurring trans fats from ruminant milk are below the reporting threshold and are confirmed zero for labelling purposes per USDA data. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.6) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1) are estimated from USDA whole milk data proportionally adjusted. 5. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated or reported values where available from USDA SR Legacy; no -1 values remain in this record. 6. The Vietnamese name Sữa Đá Chanh is preserved from the input record as the dish's original Vietnamese identity. 7. Serving size of 300 g is a standard single-glass estimate for this type of beverage in Singapore restaurant context. 8. glycemic_index set to 32 (low category) based on analogous estimate from full-fat milk GI data; gi_category is low per University of Sydney GI Database convention for dairy-based beverages.
